speaker: interviewer
text: Date-- January 25th, 1996. Survivor-- Kaz Wolff-Zdzienicki. Interviewer-- Padigail Meskin. City-- Byrne Village, Natal, country of South Africa. Language-- English. Kaz, can we pick up from where we left off, Some of the experiences that you had in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, as I told you, I was working first in the hospital. I was sick. I-- I catched typhoid or black fever, we call it. And afterwards, I was still in the hospital for a couple of weeks, recovering. And I had a, I would say, personality clash with the chief doctor in this hospital. And he kicked me out. And I went to a commander of a road builder. I was building roads. Well, till now, the roads are experienced-- the roads are in good condition. It was a good job done, even when I, after the war, 20 years after the war, I arrived by [? my ?] car. And I was driving on the road. I was proud of my job. Anyhow, sorry to say that they have been the roads gaining to the, going to the crematoriums, the main roads. Anyhow, well, and working on the roads, I saw, of course, the hundred of thousand, if not millions, of people brought to the camp and sent to, to gas chamber. They were different, from all around the world. You know, I saw many time the selection of the [? ramp-- ?] done by Mengele and other doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us how they did it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And it was left, right, left, right, left, right. To the one side, they were, they were sending people young, strong, or healthy-looking, and so on. Except when it was a young, healthy woman, and had a child with her, she was sent to the gas chamber together with the child. If she gave her child to a older woman, she had a chance to survive. Well, I was proud of the Polish girl, Polish mother, Jewish mother, who went with their children, knowing what's going on to the right, to the gas chamber. I remember-- I don't want to mention other nation who, knowing what's going on and why the selection was, was obvious, gave their child to a mother, to the grandmother, to save their life, condemning the child. Well, this was sad. But this was-- you see, everybody want to survive. I remember a story-- this, I will not repeat this what I told before about the two children burned live in the ditch. But I remember a story when I was still in the hospital. And there was a selection done by Mengele, personally by him. And there arrived the trucks. It was winter, cold. And the people who have been condemned to gas chamber had only really nothing on. Or they have been naked or in their white shirts, you know, looking like phantom. They had been loaded on the trucks and the trucks turning from the camp, from the hospital camp afterwards-- it was a hospital camp-- to the left, to the crematorium four and five that was there. And there was one Jew, I remember. It was probably a cantor from a synagogue with a very good, strong voice. He was a Austrian Jew. And he was standing on the truck, despite the wind, you know, and cold. And he sung [SPEAKING GERMAN] all the way around from the hospital to the crematorium to the gas chamber. And since this time when I heard this song, you know, I see him standing on almost over half a [INAUDIBLE] higher than the others. The others had been put down, you know, because it was cold and wind. And he was standing proud, singing this [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Well, I had a chance two or three times to save some people. You see, I was in charge afterwards as a professional road builder of a-- I had a kapo, of course, who was a Czech. I had a-- his deputy was my friend, a Polish Jew, Leon Stein. And I was a professional, I would say, engineer or technician or however you call it. And he had a group of 100 people, you know, well, a mixture of we had Russian. We had French. We had Polish Jew. We had Hungarian Jew, and Czech, and so on. And then from time to time, they have make a selection. They have done their roll call of Jews in the camp. And they passed through. If they saw somebody looking not too fit, if he had swollen foots, it was wrong-- gas chamber. And two or three times I had a chance to save somebody from my group. And when the German SS guy say, you, out. I say I'm sorry-- because I was still turning around. I was a little bit, I would say, always not too kind. I was, I was always not in the place where I should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I arrived there and say, sorry. He is high professional. I can't build roads without him. Are you say so? I say, yes. You need him? I say, it's necessary. Without him, is no road. All right, back.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was a way to save people, you know. It doesn't matter what he was doing on the road. But sometime it's-- not always, but sometime it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: The difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: It helped. Well, you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going to tell me a story about Leon, Leon Stein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon Stein. Leon Stein was really my biggest friend in Auschwitz. I was young and stupid and new in the camp. And Leon was already-- had a long, funny story of his arrest. Well, you know, it's not important for remembering. But he looked after me as a older brother. And he was experienced prisoner. It was important to have eyes on the front and on the back of your head to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he give me good advice as he looked after me. And we were very, very close together, you know. And I remember, one day arrived a transport of the liquidated ghetto from Krakow. And in this transport arrived Leon's wife and Leon's daughter. And we received information that they are-- that his wife is on the women's camp. So we managed to go there, despite we worked on the road. We managed visit people who worked on the roofs, you know, who cover the roof, because they worked there in the camp. So we bribed them a little bit. And we went there. And then I was a witness when Leon met his wife after a couple of years. And he ask, where is Helenka? Helenka was Helen, their daughter. And the wife says, oh, well, she is very well. She is now on the children's camp. We were both aware there is no children's camp in Auschwitz. It was only gas chamber for the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Leon turned his face to me, and two tears on his cheek. He understood straight away what's going on. He hasn't told to his wife, of course, this, to not have. Well, in bad luck, he survived the camp. He survived the war. He came back to Krakow. He was a reasonably weighty businessman. He was a officer of the Polish army during the war, and so on. Anyhow, he came back to Warsaw and at once-- to, to Krakow. At once, he catched jaundice and died. When I come back after two years, I met his wife only. And Leon was not alive already. Well, well, you know, there was this, what I told you. For example, till 1940-- beginning 1943, about two month before I arrive, there was no really difference between Jew and Poles and the Russian. Everybody passed selection. If somebody was sick or old-- gas chamber. Then they changed their mind on the beginning on '43, just before we arrived. The Poles, they are Aryan. The [? Aryan ?] are not selected anymore, only the Jews. Well, you know, from about 12,000 Russian, when I arrived there was alive about-- alive about 1,000. The 11,000 have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: All dead due to the typhus and things like that, or exterminated due to the selection. I had other colleagues of me in Auschwitz who went, who were arrested, say, in 1940, '41. And they told me stories about how they passed selection many times. You know, I was witness-- next to our camp, the main camp in Birkenau, was a gypsy camp. There were whole families, from little children to the very old people. And one day in summer '44, they surrounded the camp with SS people. Plenty of SS run into the camp. And the people leaving about, I say, 400 meter from the crematorium, was aware what's happen. They have been loaded on the truck, brought to the concentration-- to the crematorium, to the gas chamber-- finished, gas and smoke, gone. There was another story. There was a, as you probably heard, a Jewish township in Czechoslovakia, Theresienstadt. It was on the other side of our main camp. It was as well family camp. They had been put in the barracks, mother, father, grandmother, children, all together. And they had a reasonable good life. They received food. They were not sent to work. But after couple of months-- three months maybe-- the all the camp was put to the gas chamber straight away, like that. You know, when I arrived, there was about, I don't know, 15,000 women in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Birkenau, yes. Then at once, with a whole big transport from Hungary and so on, they soared to about 60,000. But then it started going down, down, down, down. They received very poor food, no medical attention at all. And what who survived was put to the gas chamber. So I don't know, maybe in total survived from this 100,000 women who passed, 150,000 approximately, as we are checking now after the war of women through, it's mainly Jewish woman and Polish and all other-- maybe 10,000, maybe less. But look, from my transport, 406. The Red Cross checked after the war how many survived. They find out that we have been not passing any selection. And the Red Cross find out apparently, as I was told, 18 of us only to survive. Because despite we receive food parcel from home, there was such a quantity of disease and illness in the camp and a lack of medicine that pneumonia, TB-- I don't speak about heart attack. This is not important. Heart attack is for rich people, you know, in good condition, overeating and so on. But here, typhus, typhoid, dysentery, malaria-- they decimated the people, you see. A barrack of 400 of us had daily about six to 10 dead every day. If you would not receive new people to the barrack, after about, I say, two months, there would be the barrack empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you get new transports in?

speaker: interviewee
text: What say?

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you get transports coming in of new people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, it's almost every day, almost every day. Well, there have been periods when you had train after train coming. That's what I told you. The capacity of the concen-- of the gas chambers was 36,000. And there were days when they arrived more-- 40,000, 45,000 a day, you know, long cattle train, about 60 cattle trucks, in each one, about 50, 60 people. That's about 3,500 in one train. And it was train after train after train, day and night. When they cleaned the ghettos, for example, in Hungary-- I remember '44, June-- it was traffic like hell. And they, they increased the quantity of the Sonderkommando. It was a kommando consisted only of Jews who worked in the crematoriums. They had a horrible job. They had to take the people into the gas chamber, take the corpses from the gas chamber to the oven, dispose afterwards of the ashes, and this whole job. And this was, when I arrived in '43, the group was about 50, 60 people like that. But it was only one crematorium working. Afterwards, when it was four crematoriums, because the number one was Auschwitz, main camp, and two, three, four, and five was in Birkenau. The Sonderkommando was increased to 5- 600 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, what's happened? Some people, most of their people have been selected by force and sent to work in the Sonderkommando. Only few volunteered to go, because it was better food. And they could organize something from the goods which the people brought with them to the gas chamber, and so. But it was a rather, I would say, a risky business. Because from time to time, they have taken a group of Sonderkommando and sent to another camp, for example to Mauthausen or to another camp. And they have been put in the gas chamber there and finished, not to leave any witness to the job. Why, for example, the Gestapo, the SS, have put dynamite below the old crematoriums and today, we got rubbles only? They were thinking that they destroy the evidence. I rather see this idea. It's true. Could you tell me about your experience and relationship with Colonel Boger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, with Boger I had twice, I would say, personality clash. One day, we worked-- it was winter-- we came back from work. And I was working building a road close, in the hospital area, close to the big, I would say, warehouses where worked a special kommando who selected the goods from their transport. And there was different things you get, from gold to jewelry to food and clothing, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So through the barbed wire between hospital camp and the-- the warehouse barracks, we have made some businesses, you know. They had been every day searched, almost every day, not to bring anything in the camp. So they throw to us. And we brought to the camp. And then we received some, I would say, interest, how you call it, you know. Sometimes it was food. Sometimes it was, say, for example, gold or jewelry or a watch or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one day, one of my acquaintance prisoner working in the warehouse throw me, through the barbed wire, a gold coin. And I had this gold coin in my hand when I walked back into the camp. And we passed a gate. And we had to report. I say, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And then you give the quantity of how many-- 100 [GERMAN] 100 prisoners. And then we passed. And the Boger stayed there and looks on us, says, kommando, stop. We stopped. He looked on the faces, look on the face. You, and take me out. And I had, in my right hand, I had a cap. We have to put the cap down when you go through the gate before the SS. And I got this gold coin. And he ordered me, take me out. The kommando is standing. He say me, dress off. Put that all down. It was winter-- cold like hell, but nothing to do. But it was snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I have done, I have put this gold coin into the snow close to my shoe. And I take the shoe off. He takes the shoe, puts his hand inside if I got nothing in-- no, then jacket and everything. I was naked. And then my, I dressed myself again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And slowly, I put the gold coin back in my hand and walk out in the camp and finish. This was-- so I survived. And I brought a gold coin into the camp. And I, afterwards, I saw the chap after the roll call who gave me this, and we have made some business, you know. Because you could buy-- they have, they worked plenty of civilian in the camp, supervising technical jobs. And with them, you could make business. It's mean you gave him a money or a piece of jewelry and he brought you bread, you know, or sausage. Of course, it cost a fortune. For example, a half a bottle of vodka costed-- I remember very well-- $15. So it was very expensive at this time. Anyhow, and the second time-- and this, I wrote a memoir called I was working in a, in a group of making boreholes. It was far from the camp, about 45 kilometers walking one way and another. And we worked there. And it was a pleasant job. Of course, we had two guards with dogs, and finish. And we are, we are a group of five, six of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I was just working, taking the soil out, which was came from the bottom. And I had no shirt. It was hot in summer. And I heard a German voice behind me-- hey, you. I turn out. It was Boger. And then, why are you without shirt? I said, well, it's so hot. But I forgot to take my cap down.

speaker: interviewee
text: He catch my cap and throw this cap behind the guardsmen who were sitting and supervising or standing, supervising us. And this was a death sentence, because if I go behind the guards, I pass the line of security. They supposed to kill me as a prisoner who tried to escape. If I don't go, I don't follow the order of Boger. Then he will kill me, because I don't follow the order. Because he throw the cap and say, go and fetch. I realize what's going on. Well, I would say I was dead frozen at this moment. But thank god, the chief of this group was a young student, Austrian. He walked through the line, safety line, catched my cap, brought back, throw the cap into my, into me, gave me a kick. Why are you not working, in German, of course. So I was relaxed, of course. And then I started working. And then Boger told me, oh, don't worry. I will catch you. I will catch you. He walked away. But eventually, after a couple of years, one day when I was interrogation officer in the American army, then I walked into the main camp in Dachau, where at this time was about 41,000 SS and war criminals. And there was a group ready to discharged, waiting to go out for a discharge camp. I walked along. And I see somebody. Ah, I know him very well. I realize straight away whom I see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I say, come here. What are you doing here? He came out. He reported with full rank and name, [GERMAN] Friedrich Wilhelm Boger [GERMAN]. I said, OK, come with me. I told to the officer, sergeant in charge of the group. I say, well listen, I take this out. Don't worry. I brought him to the prison straight away. I run to the chief of prosecution I say, well, listen, what are you doing? I found Boger. And you got him on the list of war criminals. And he was for discharge. He said, impossible. I said, but look. He take the list of-- well, the book really-- of war criminals. And he said, yes. Where you got him? I said, well, I put him in the prison. OK, good. You will interrogate him. So I was pleased only. After a couple of days, I arrived. And see, I was a-- I had a maybe not so human approach. I interrogated only nighttime. I started interrogation about 9:00, 10:00 o'clock evenings when they already were asleep. So I called the sergeant in charge. I said, wake him up. Bring him to interrogation cell. And always, I was there and a secretary who recorded his deposition. And then Boger arrived. And I ask him, tell me your story. He started with plenty of lies, that he was in administration, and this and that. And I heard this. And I say, well, you've this, this what you have to say? Yes. Then I will remember-- I will remind you of some things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him some stories from Auschwitz, even the story with the cap. He say. He looks and he say, ah, yes, yes. Your face [GERMAN]. Your face is so known to me. I am sorry I haven't killed you at this time. Ah, I was furious, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.
